figures.caption: Table 1: Performance comparison of LLaVA-1.5-7B [27] under different pruning ratios. The best and second-best results are bolded and underlined, respectively, and the last column shows the average accuracy relative to the upper bound.

Table 2: Performance comparison of LLaVA-NeXT-7B [28] under different pruning ratios. The best and second-best results are bolded and underlined, respectively, and the last column shows the average accuracy relative to the upper bound.
Figure 5: Performance comparison of various methods on LLaVA-1.5-7B across multiple benchmarks at different pruning ratios.

Figure 9:Qualitative comparison of SparseVLM [47], HoloV [48], FlowCut [35], and our proposedmethodDeSAP on the POPE dataset [22]. The figure presents original images alongside their pruned versions at pruning ratios of 88.9%, 77.8%, and 66.7%. Bounding boxes are used to highlight key semantic regions aligned with the text. Our method demonstrates superior preservation of key semantics, especially under high pruning ratios (e.g., 88.9%).

Figure 2: Attention analysis in LVLMs. (a) [CLS] attention and cross-attention for pruning are biased toward global semantics and text-adjacent regions, respectively. (b) We separate detail-preserving attention from global semantic residuals by decomposing the hidden state. (c) Vanilla similarity dominated by global semantics, while our decoupled similarity accurately identifies task-relevant local regions.

Figure 8:Qualitative comparison of SparseVLM [47], HoloV [48], FlowCut [35], and our proposedmethod DeSAP on the GQA dataset [18]. The figure presents original images alongside their pruned versions at pruning ratios of 88.9%, 77.8%, and 66.7%. Bounding boxes are used to highlight key semantic regions aligned with the text. Our method demonstrates superior preservation of key semantics, especially under high pruning ratios (e.g., 88.9%).

Figure 6: Visualization on GQA comparing SparseVLM, HoloV, and Ours. The original image and the corresponding pruned results are shown at pruning ratios of 88.9%, 77.8%, and 66.7%. Gray areas indicate discarded tokens, while red regions highlight the primary task-relevant semantic tokens. Compared with other methods, our method well preserves critical tokens.

Figure 3: Decoupled Similarity for Token Pruning. (a) Global bias in visual-centric pruning methods and limitations of two variant strategies. (b) Current task centric pruning methods fails to leverage textual guidance. (c) Our decoupled similarity better localizes task-related regions and effectively exploits dual information for token pruning. (d) Evaluation results validate the effectiveness of decoupled similarity and its ability to integrate visual cues for dual-source guided token pruning.

Figure 7: Qualitative comparison of activation maps from [CLS] attention, cross-attention, vanilla similarity, and our decoupled similarity. While the first three exhibit noticeable biases, our method consistently achieves precise localization aligned with the text-query semantics, demonstrating its effectiveness for encoder-side semantic alignment in token pruning.

Figure 4: Overall Architecture of DeSAP, a novel Decoupled Similarity-Aware Pruning method. DeSAP computes decoupled similarity by combining decoupled attention with text via fine-grained cross-modal token alignment, enabling precise task guidance within the encoder. It is further integrated with [CLS] attention with equal weighting for dual-source guidance token pruning. Non-selected tokens are aggregated with their most similar neighbors via averaging.
